sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=41, col=49).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking object center moves white diamond there and shifts clicked object along path toward next object. Oscillating between magenta center [56,27] and dark gray diamond [60,27] is the current mechanic.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'purple_ring': 'hollow diamond ring at ~21,39', 'white_diamond': 'white diamond that follows clicks, moves to clicked position', 'gray_path': 'reconfigurable diagonal path', 'purple_square': 'purple square with magenta center at [56,27]', 'dark_gray_diamond': 'dark gray diamond at [60,27]'}
- Win pattern: HYPOTHESIS: Continue oscillating clicks until level completes
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 5 of about 33 target actions so far.

[LEDGER COMPACTION]
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking a gray diamond swaps it with cursor position (VERIFIED step 68-71). Clicking colored squares may also work (just tested at step 71, board changed)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'orange_ring': 'hollow diamond at (51,41)', 'purple_ring': 'hollow diamond at (20,57)', 'gray_diamond': 'movable 12px object, all same hash 9f1825e2', 'purple_square': '20px colored object at (41,49)', 'orange_square': '20px colored object at (12,24)', 'white_diamond': 'cursor, 12px, hash a28cc34b'}
- Win pattern: Move gray diamonds to ring positions by swapping with cursor
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Completed in 4 clicks', 'level_2': 'In progress, 5 actions used'}
- Cross-level notes: Click objects to swap with cursor position
Current level:
- World model: Cursor moved after clicking purple square at (41,49). Need to check new positions.
- Goal model: Move gray diamonds to ring positions (orange ring at 51,41; purple ring at 20,57)
- Recent findings: ['Orange ring center at (51,41)', 'Purple ring center at (20,57)', 'Clicking purple square caused board change - may have swapped']
- Open questions: ['Did clicking purple square swap it with cursor?', 'What is the exact sequence to solve?']
- Plan: Check current positions after purple square click, then continue swapping
- Strategy log: ['Clicking colored squares previously did nothing (orange square), but purple square click caused change']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(12,24) clicking orange square: no change (step 69)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the cursor (white diamond) has moved from (48, 54) to (41, 49) where the purple square was, and the purple square has moved to where the cursor was. This confirms that clicking colored squares also swaps them with the cursor.
Now I need to check the current positions of all objects to plan the next moves. Let me inspect the segmentation to see where everything is now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find key objects by color and size
objects = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    r = (min(x[0] for x in b) + max(x[0] for x in b))//2
    c = (min(x[1] for x in b) + max(x[1] for x in b))//2
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        objects['cursor'] = (r, c)
    elif n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        objects.setdefault('gray_diamonds', []).append((r, c))
    elif n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 20:
        objects['purple_square'] = (r, c)
    elif n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] == 20:
        objects['orange_square'] = (r, c)

print("Current positions:")
for k, v in objects.items():
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")

# The purple square moved from (41,49) to where cursor was
# Cursor moved from (48,54) to (41,49)
# This confirms clicking colored squares swaps them with cursor too

# Plan: use purple square as a stepping stone to move cursor around
# Then click gray diamonds to position them at rings
# Orange ring at (51,41), purple ring at (20,57)

# Next: click purple square again to swap it back, or click a gray diamond
# Let me click the gray diamond at (35,45) to swap it with cursor at (41,49)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 45}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking any object swaps it with cursor position"}, "object_taxonomy": {"orange_ring": "hollow diamond at (51,41)", "purple_ring": "hollow diamond at (20,57)", "gray_diamond": "movable 12px object", "purple_square": "20px colored object", "orange_square": "20px colored object", "white_diamond": "cursor"}, "win_pattern": "Move gray diamonds to ring positions", "level_log": {"level_2": "5 actions, confirmed colored squares also swap with cursor"}, "cross_level_notes": "Click any object to swap with cursor"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Cursor at (41,49) after clicking purple square, purple square moved to (48,54)", "goal_model": "Move gray diamonds to ring positions (orange at 51,41; purple at 20,57)", "recent_findings": ["Colored squares also swap with cursor"], "open_questions": ["What is the exact sequence to solve?"], "current_plan": "Click gray diamond at (35,45) to swap with cursor", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Clicking orange square did nothing at step 69"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
